summary: I left from Louisville, Kentucky when I was inducted into the Army. That’s where I went for my induction process before heading to basic training. Louisville was the starting point of my military journey, and I remember them telling me they’d see me in a month or so. It was a big moment leaving home from there.

I had hoped to join the United States Air Force because I thought their training would be more applicable to civilian life. An Air Force doctor even examined my knees and asked why I was in the Army, suggesting I might have had better luck getting into the Air Force. But ultimately, I ended up in the Army and not the Air Force. I also knew a guy from Newburgh, Indiana who had been in the Air Force before joining the Army and volunteering for Vietnam.

I took my basic training at Fort Knox, which was a mix of National Guard and regular Army trainees. After basic, I stayed at Fort Knox for reconnaissance scout training, which was very tough and demanding. Fort Knox was where I learned vital skills like map reading, camouflage, and artillery adjustment. It was a key place in my military preparation before going overseas.

I was originally scheduled to go to Fort Bliss, Texas, for missile school after basic training. However, during training, I got a foot infection from the rain and couldn’t transfer until my profile expired. Eventually, my orders to Fort Bliss were pulled, and I stayed at Fort Knox instead for reconnaissance scout training. So, I never actually went to Fort Bliss.

I remember a guy from Newburgh, Indiana, who had previously served in the Air Force and then joined the Army. He volunteered to go to Vietnam but was instead sent to Germany, which I thought was ironic. He was one of the few people I knew personally who had a strong desire to serve in Vietnam. Newburgh was mentioned in connection with his military path.

My father was a World War II veteran who landed at Omaha Beach on D-Day. He didn’t talk much about his experiences, but I learned a lot from my mom and from Time Life books on World War II that I got after I married. My dad would sometimes point out his unit insignia in photos and tell me about being in Luxembourg and Germany. His service shaped some of my views on military duty.

Omaha Beach was where my father landed during World War II on D-Day. He carried shrapnel from that battle and had firsthand recollections, though he rarely spoke about it. I only really understood the significance of Omaha Beach because of a schoolteacher who also participated in D-Day and would talk about it at school assemblies. Omaha Beach was a key part of my dad’s military story.

D-Day was a significant event in my father’s life during World War II when he landed at Omaha Beach. He was on the third wave, and a schoolteacher who spoke at our school was on the seventeenth wave, which created some tension. My father didn’t glorify his experience but it was clear it affected him deeply. D-Day was a defining moment in his military service and influenced my understanding of war.

My father was stationed in Luxembourg during World War II, and he would sometimes point out where he had been on a map or in photos. He didn’t talk much about it, but I knew he was there from what he shared and from my mom’s stories. Luxembourg was part of the European theater where he served before moving on to Germany. It was a piece of his military journey I came to appreciate later.

Germany was another place where my father served during World War II after Luxembourg. He would sometimes point out locations on maps or in books, showing me where he had been. I learned about his time in Germany mostly through my mom and later through reading Time Life books on the war. Germany was part of the path he took during his military service.

After I got married, I bought the Time Life books on World War II, which helped me understand my father’s experiences better. He would come over and look at the pictures, sometimes pointing out his unit insignia or where he had been. Those books were a valuable resource for connecting with my dad’s past and learning more about the war. Time Life helped me bridge the gap in stories my father rarely shared.

I was drafted during the Vietnam War and eventually sent there after my training. The war wasn’t openly discussed much at my school or in my community, though there were some casualties and a few people I knew who served. I trained as a reconnaissance scout and went through Vietnam-specific training before deployment. My time in Vietnam was a defining part of my military service and life experience.

At Fort Knox, I went through reconnaissance scout training, which was very demanding and covered a wide range of skills like camouflage, explosives, map reading, and artillery adjustment. The training emphasized stealth and intelligence gathering rather than direct fighting. We had to complete a tough final test involving a timed ten-mile run with various tasks. This training prepared me for the complex missions I would face in Vietnam.

I flew out of Oakland, California when I left the United States for Vietnam. It was my first experience at O'Hare Airport, and luckily my mother’s second cousin was on the same flight, which helped me navigate the airport. We barely made our connecting flight, which was a bit stressful. Oakland was the departure point for my overseas deployment.

My mother’s second cousin was traveling from Evansville, Indiana, to Green Bay, Wisconsin, for business and happened to be on the same flight out of Evansville as me. He helped me get through O'Hare Airport, which was a big help since I was unfamiliar with it. Evansville was the starting location for that part of my journey to Vietnam.

My mother’s second cousin was flying to Green Bay, Wisconsin, for business when he was on the same flight as me out of Evansville. He helped me navigate the airport during our layover at O'Hare. Green Bay was his destination, and it played a small but helpful role in my travel story to Vietnam.

O'Hare International Airport was my first experience there during my trip to Vietnam. I had a tight connection and was unfamiliar with the airport, but my mother’s second cousin helped me get through it. We barely made the connecting flight, which was stressful but memorable. O'Hare was a key transit point on my way overseas.

When I arrived in Vietnam, I landed at Bien Hoa Air Base, which was hot, dirty, and had mostly low buildings that looked like sheds or huts. The heat and smells hit you immediately, but you adjust quickly over time. Bien Hoa was my first impression of Vietnam and the place where I started my deployment. It was a tough environment but one I learned to live with.

Long Binh was one of the places mentioned in relation to my Vietnam service, though I didn’t elaborate much about it in the interview. It was part of the area where troops were stationed during the war. Long Binh was one of the key military locations I was connected to during my time in Vietnam.

Nha Trang was another location associated with my service in Vietnam, though I didn’t go into detail about it in the interview. It was one of the places where American forces operated during the war. Nha Trang was part of the broader context of my deployment in Vietnam.
